contradiction_text: March 3 was described as a broad cross-service degradation, but the actual impact was sharply uneven across services.
related_claim_texts: The March 3 incident was a broad cross-service degradation, with peak failures around 40% for github.com and 43% for the GitHub API, while impact was materially lower for Git over HTTP, Copilot, and GitHub Actions.
The March 3 event was platform-wide in scope but not in severity.
disclosure: Keep visible that the outage was broad in scope but not uniform in severity; SSH was unaffected and Actions stayed under 1% impact.

contradiction_text: The March 5 Actions failure was caused by changes presented as resiliency updates, so work intended to improve resilience instead introduced the routing fault.
related_claim_texts: {'Later March incidents were severe but service-specific': 'GitHub Actions delays on March 5 were tied to a Redis load-balancer misconfiguration, while the March 19-20 Copilot Coding Agent failures were tied to authentication problems, and the second Copilot incident followed incomplete remediation of the first.'}
GitHub tied the March 5 Actions failure to a Redis load-balancer misconfiguration introduced during resiliency updates, with internal traffic routed to the wrong host.
disclosure: Do not smooth away that the failure emerged from resiliency-oriented changes rather than from an external shock or unrelated defect class.

contradiction_text: GitHub said it mitigated the first Copilot Coding Agent incident by rotating credentials, yet a second and worse outage followed because remediation was incomplete.
related_claim_texts: {'Later March incidents were severe but service-specific': 'GitHub Actions delays on March 5 were tied to a Redis load-balancer misconfiguration, while the March 19-20 Copilot Coding Agent failures were tied to authentication problems, and the second Copilot incident followed incomplete remediation of the first.'}
GitHub said the second Copilot incident happened because remediation after the first was incomplete, which turns the sequence into a remediation-quality failure as much as an authentication failure.
disclosure: Keep visible that the recurrence was not just the same fault repeating; the reported mitigation after the first incident did not fully resolve the underlying exposure, and the second incident was materially worse.

contradiction_text: The March 24 Teams integration failure was counted within GitHub's availability reporting even though the stated cause was an upstream dependency outage outside GitHub's fully described internal mechanism.
related_claim_texts: GitHub reported four incidents in March 2026 that degraded availability across different parts of its platform.

{"A fourth March 24 incident hit GitHub's Microsoft Teams integrations": 'average error rate was 37.4%, peak error rate was 90.1%, and about 19% of installs failed to receive notifications, with GitHub attributing the break to an upstream dependency outage.'}
disclosure: Keep visible the accountability tension: the incident belongs in GitHub availability experience for users, but the mechanism is bounded by an upstream dependency GitHub did not fully explain.
